explanation.rule: The suffix -ist forms nouns referring to a person who holds a particular belief, follows a profession, or practises an art or skill. It commonly pairs with -ism (the corresponding doctrine or system). A related variant, -ian/-ician, follows the same pattern for some professions.

groups.note: Attaches to nouns or adjective stems to form a person noun. A related variant -ian/-ician is used for some professions (e.g. music → musician). Many -ist words pair with a corresponding -ism form (nationalist → nationalism).

examples.definition: a person who campaigns vigorously for social or political change
a person with expert knowledge in a particular field
a person who accepts situations as they are and acts accordingly
a person who studies or practises a branch of science
a person with strong loyalty to and promotion of their own country

wordForms.definition: a person who campaigns energetically for social, political, or environmental change
wordForms.example: The climate activist chained herself to the gates of the parliament building.

wordForms.definition: a person who creates paintings, sculptures, or other works of creative expression
wordForms.example: The artist's latest exhibition challenged conventional ideas about identity.
wordForms.rootDefinition: the expression or application of creative skill and imagination

wordForms.definition: a person who writes for newspapers, magazines, or broadcast news
wordForms.example: The investigative journalist uncovered evidence of widespread corruption.
wordForms.rootDefinition: a daily record of events; a periodical publication

wordForms.definition: a person who plays a musical instrument or composes music professionally
wordForms.example: The musician had performed in concert halls across four continents.
wordForms.rootDefinition: vocal or instrumental sounds combined to produce beauty and expression

wordForms.definition: a person who advocates strongly for the interests and independence of their own country
wordForms.example: The nationalist movement gained considerable support in the run-up to the referendum.
wordForms.rootDefinition: a large community of people sharing a common language, culture, or territory

wordForms.definition: a person who tends to be hopeful and positive about future outcomes
wordForms.example: As an optimist, he always believed a solution could be found.
wordForms.rootDefinition: hopefulness and confidence about the future

wordForms.definition: a person who habitually expects the worst and sees the negative side of things
wordForms.example: Even the most hardened pessimist admitted that the results were better than expected.
wordForms.rootDefinition: a tendency to see the worst in situations

wordForms.definition: a person who faces facts and situations as they are, without illusion
wordForms.example: As a realist, she knew the project would require far more resources than planned.
wordForms.rootDefinition: actually existing or occurring; not imagined or idealised

wordForms.definition: a person who is trained in and works in a field of science
wordForms.example: The scientist published groundbreaking research on antibiotic resistance.
wordForms.rootDefinition: the systematic study of the physical and natural world through observation and experiment

wordForms.definition: a person with deep expertise or professional training in a specific area
wordForms.example: The hospital referred her to a specialist in tropical diseases.
wordForms.rootDefinition: better or greater than usual; designed for a particular purpose

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: The human rights ___ was awarded an international prize for her work.
The doctor recommended seeing a ___ before undergoing any further treatment.
The ___ refused to romanticise the situation and insisted on addressing the facts.
The ___ movement demanded a separate parliament for the region.
An accomplished ___, she had recorded five albums before the age of thirty.
The ___ presented her findings at the annual conference on climate change.

transformExercises.sentence: The renowned ___ donated several works to the national gallery. (ART)
She was a committed ___ who had campaigned for renewable energy for years. (ACTIVE)
Even the biggest ___ in the room acknowledged that the outlook was bleak. (OPTIMISM)
The ___ was asked to testify before the parliamentary committee. (SCIENCE)
The hospital's leading ___ in cardiology performed the surgery. (SPECIAL)
